coredata.dc:title: Conjugate area-controlled synthesis of multiple-color carbon dots and application in sensors and optoelectronic devices

Zhang, Hao
Yu, Nanhai
Sun, Zhonghui
Chen, Liang
coredata.dc:description: During the heretofore several years, Carbon dots (CDs) have achieved the widely used in fluorescence sensing, optoelectronics and bio imaging owing to their distinctive photoluminescence attribute. Here, CDs with bule to red emission were prepared by solvent heat treatment of phenol-derived with regulation the reaction time in the synthetic system, and their fluorescence spectra ranged from 450 ∼ 650 nm. Characterization through variety of methods and density functional theory calculations clearly demonstrates that the achieve stable long-wavelength emission caused in solvent is mainly due to the increase in conjugate area and oxygen elements of CDs. Importantly, conjugate area and oxidation degree can affect the level of HOMO-LUMO, further changing the band gap leading to fluorescent red-shift. Because of their excellent photoluminescent properties, we examined the performance in detection of non-metal ions by green and yellow-color CDs. The results show that they have high accuracy and rapid reaction to ClO− within the scope of 0 ∼ 50 μM and 4 ∼ 44 μM, with response limits are 31.1 nM and 56.5 nM, respectively. Moreover, we also prepared CDs/PMMA films and CDs/epoxy resin films with highly transparent and stability. Subsequently, the white-light emission diodes (0.32, 0.31; ClE coordinate) was obtained through mixing CDs in a certain proportion. This study provides a novel platform for fluorescence sensing of multi-color emission CDs, and provides strong potential for new optoelectronic equipment.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Conjugate Area-Controlled Synthesis of Multiple-Color Carbon Dots and Application in Sensors and Optoelectronic Devices

During the heretofore several years, Carbon dots (CDs) have achieved the widely used in fluorescence sensing, optoelectronics and bio imaging owing to their distinctive photoluminescence attribute. Here, CDs with bule to red emission were prepared by solvent heat treatment of phenol-derived with regulation the reaction time in the synthetic system, and their fluorescence spectra ranged from 450 ∼ 650 nm. Characterization through variety of methods and density functional theory calculations clearly demonstrates that the achieve stable long-wavelength emission caused in solvent is mainly due to the increase in conjugate area and oxygen elements of CDs. Importantly, conjugate area and oxidation degree can affect the level of HOMO-LUMO, further changing the band gap leading to fluorescent red-shift. Because of their excellent photoluminescent properties, we examined the performance in detection of non-metal ions by green and yellow-color CDs. The results show that they have high accuracy and rapid reaction to ClO− within the scope of 0 ∼ 50 μM and 4 ∼ 44 μM, with response limits are 31.1 nM and 56.5 nM, respectively. Moreover, we also prepared CDs/PMMA films and CDs/epoxy resin films with highly transparent and stability. Subsequently, the white-light emission diodes (0.32, 0.31; ClE coordinate) was obtained through mixing CDs in a certain proportion. This study provides a novel platform for fluorescence sensing of multi-color emission CDs, and provides strong potential for new optoelectronic equipment.

ce:section.ce:para: CDs is a new type of carbon nanomaterials that bring excellent light-emitting properties to carbon nanomaterials, making them widely attention in the fluorescence field. [1,2] Due to its excellent optical properties, it has received extensive attention in the potential fields such as biological imaging, photoelectric conversion, fluorescence detection and light emitting devices (LED) et. since CDs were discovered in 2004 [3,4]. In the initial work, the emission wavelength of CDs was mainly concentrated in the blue region and had low photoluminescence (PL) quantum yields (QYs), that largely limited its further application. [5,6] For more than a decade, researchers have tried prepare CDs with longer wavelength emission and high QYs by various methods, which includes changing the preparation method (solvothermal method, microwave method, pyrolysis method, arc discharge method, electrochemical oxidation method, etc.); inducing better charge/carrier transport through surface passivation and heteroatom doping methods and find more suitable precursors, etc. [7–9] For instance, using expensive polythiophene phenylpropionic acid as a precursor, Wang and co-workers developed hydrothermal reaction processes to prepare red emissive CDs with a low QYs of 2.3 %. [10] Yuan et al. prepared the fluorescence-adjustable CDs with QYs of 30 ∼ 40 %, based on different solvothermal treatment time of citric acid and diaminonaphthalene isomers [11]. Utilizing citric acid and thiourea as raw material, Qu et al. developed S- and N-dopedred emissive CDs with enhanced electron delocalizatio, QYs is 8 % [12]. Ding and his team synthesized red light-emitting CDs with QYs of 24 %. [13] The red launch of CDs was implemented via adjusting the surface states on the precursors p-phenylenediamine and urea. Although these efforts, the synthesis of multicolor-emission CDs (M-CDs) is still limited by the expensive precursors, complicated synthesis steps, and low QYs. Meanwhile, there is a lack of clear understanding of the formation mechanism and process of carbon dots and the luminescence mechanism. Therefore, it is very necessary to explore the easy synthetic route of M-CDs and clear explanation of its luminescence mechanism and as well as research its application in Sensors and Optoelectronic Devices.

Owing to the merits of long service life, significant energy saving effect and soft light, white light emitting diodes (WLEDs) stand out among numerous lighting equipment. [14] More importantly, because their advantages of comfortable ambient lighting and avoiding eye fatigue, warm-color WLEDs lighting systems with low correlated color temperature (CCT) and high color rendering index (CRI > 80) can be used for indoor lighting. [15–17]. Traditional rare earth materials are expensive in the research of new WLEDs, and WLEDs based on Cd2+ or Pb2+ quantum dots are toxic and harmful to people and the environment. [18,19] Therefore, environmentally friendly and low-cost CDs have become the ideal choice for manufacturing WLEDs. However, intense blue light can cause retinal damage, while, CDs will quench in the powder fettle, which greatly limits their application in light-emitting devices. Ideally, WLEDs should be designed to excite a suitable combination of wide-emitting red/green/blue tri-color phosphors to improve CRI and color stability, especially to reduce the damage of blue peaks in white light to eyes. [20–22] The purpose of effectively suppressing resonance energy transfer and π-π interaction between CDs can be achieved through encapsulating CDs into the polymer matrix. This strategy can reduce the aggregation-induced quenched of CDs and retain most of the solid fluorescence [23,24]. Therefore, there is great potential for selecting suitable raw materials to prepare CDs, and packaging them into solid materials to develop warm-colored WELDs.

In this work, the distinct multicolor-emission CDs (M-CDs) were synthesized through onestep solvothermal method using phenol-derived (catechol, m-dihydroxybenzene, hydroquinone, 1,6-dihydroxynaphthalene). Through a series of characterizations, it was proved that the fluorescence red shift of CDs is caused by the increase of conjugate area and the increase of oxygen element. In addition, careful Density Functional Theory (DFT) theoretical calculations confirm that the prepared M-CDs have the advantages of high charge dispersion and high structural stability. Subsequently, based on the excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity, discussing non-metal ions detection capacity by green and yellow-color CDs. The results show that CDs have a wide detection range, high accuracy, rapid sensing and can be used as fluorescence sensors for ClO− ion detection. Furthermore, M-CDs have been successfully used in the manufacture of M-CDs fluorescent films and M-CDs/epoxy resin films, and white-light LEDs due to their outstanding photostability. It is expected that such multiple-color CDs will have a good application prospect in anti-counterfeiting technology, and fluorescent sensing and photoelectric facilities.

Catechol (98 %), m-dihydroxybenzene (98 %), hydroquinone (98 %), 1,6-dihydroxynaphthalene and polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical ReagentCo., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Epoxy resin and H2SO4 (98 %) were buy from Komiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). The organic solvents, i.e. MeOH, DMF, EtOH and DCM were gained from Tianjin Huihang Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). The fetal bovine serum was purchased from Yuhengfeng Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). All reagents and solvents weren’t undergone any other treatment before use.

Catechol (0.50 g, 4.54 mmol), m-dihydroxybenzene (0.50 g, 4.54 mmol), hydroquinone (0.50 g, 4.54 mmol), 1,6-dihydroxynaphthalene (0.50 g, 3.12 mmol) respectively dissolved in 30 mL EtOH and sonicate for 30 min, after add 2 mL of H2SO4 and continue sonication for 10 min. Then the mixtures were then transferred to 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon)-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C with different reaction time. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting product through centrifuge to remove insoluble impurities, then removed solvent by rotary evaporation under vacuum. Finally, further purified via silica column chromatography to obtain pure M-CDs (Eluent volume ratio: DCM/MeOH: 30/1). (To further explain the related mechanism of M-CDs, the CDs prepared by hydroquinone, resorcinol, and 1,6-naphthalene are used as raw materials and heated for 2 h, resorcinol and 1,6-naphthalenediphenol and heated for 7 h were respectively labeled as B-CDs, G-CDs, O-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs.)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in sensors and optoelectronic devices
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of ClO− was performed at room temperature in EtOH/Tris buffer (V/V:1/1, pH = 7.0). All the test through the following steps. Use pipette to add the 200 μL CDs preparation solution to 10 mL volumetric flask, then add a certain amount of ClO− preparation solution, and use EtOH/Tris buffer to make the volume constant. After fully shaking the resulting solution, stand still at room temperature for 30 min., and then measure its fluorescence emission spectrum. To avoid errors and mistakes, all these experiments were conducted three control experiments. The detailed preparation process of the fluorescent films and Light-Emitting diodes can be found in the supporting literature.

As introduced in the experimental section, M-CDs are prepared by one-step solvothermal treatment. As carbon precursor, phenol derivatives have a unique symmetric structure. In a single molecule, adjacent sites activated by electron-donating hydroxyl groups have highly reactive hydrogen atoms, which can be sequential dehydrative condensation with the electron donor hydroxyl group to increase conjugate the π domain is used to synthesize CDs to achieve fluorescence red-shift. [25] One molecule of phenol-derived could be considered as the smallest sp2 domain with −OH groups, which concomitantly acts as a building block to form large sized conjugated sp2 cluster with −OH groups at the edge sites by controllable solvothermal approach at the strong oxidation of H2SO4 [26,27]. In addition, the emission wavelength of the CDs can also be adjusted by controlling the heating time to regulation the degree of dehydration condensation of the raw materials. These are the key points for the synthesis of the M-CDs (Scheme 1 ).

Due to the unique symmetric structure of the carbon precursor, the dehydration condensation and graphitization steps that occur during the formation of M-CDs can be predicted (Scheme 2 ). To investigate the formation mechanism, the fluorescence spectra of CDs prepared at 200 °C with different solvothermal times and different raw materials were studied (Table S1). And the fluorescence spectra of the synthesized carbon dots were studied. When catechol and hydroquinone are used as raw materials, with the solvothermal times increases, the fluorescence of the prepared CDs does not show a significant red shift, but m-dihydroxybenzene and 1,6-dihydroxynaphthalene are used as raw materials, with the extension of heating times, the emission wavelength of the obtained CDs illustrate significant red-shift. However, when the heating time was extended to 11 h, the fluorescence of the obtained carbon dots did not show any further red shift (Figure S1). This result shows that the emission wavelength of the CDs is influenced by both the position of the hydroxyl group on the raw materials and the solvothermal times. When the two hydroxyl groups on the raw material are in the ortho or para position, the hydrogen in adjacent positions cannot be activated by two hydroxyl groups of the electron donor at the same time, so the hydrogen activity at this position is low, which is not conducive to continuous dehydration condensation in the solvothermal process. [28–30] Therefore, extending the heating time does not increase the conjugate area of the prepared CDs. When the two hydroxyl groups on the raw material are in the meta position or exist in the form of naphthol, the hydrogen at the adjacent position is simultaneously activated by the hydroxyl groups of the two electron donors. Therefore, the hydrogen at this position is highly reactive, which is beneficial to the solvothermal process. The continuous dehydration condensation in the medium leads to the increase of the conjugate area of CDs with the increase of solvothermal time. Based on this mechanism, we can theoretically prepare CDs with the required emission wavelength by changing the raw material and solvothermal time.

As illustrate in Figs. 1 a-e, the size of M-CDs is uniform, which can be obtained from transmission electron microscope (TEM) images. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images clearly reveal that the M-CDs with high degree of graphitization and spacing of 0. 21 nm (insets of Fig. 1a-e). This result is consistent with the graphite carbon crystal plane (100). As can be seen from the particle size distribution diagrams (Fig. 1f-j), the average particle sizes of B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, O-CDs, and R-CDs increased in order and were: 2.4894 nm, 2.7392 nm, 2.9372 nm, 3.0086 nm and 3.1518 nm. This shows that the conjugate area of R-CDs is the largest, the conjugate area of B-CDs is the smallest, and the fluorescence red-shift of M-CDs is caused by the increase of its conjugate area. [31] Fig. 2 a is fluorescence emission spectrum of M-CDs (From B-CDs to R-CDs, the excitation wavelengths are 400 nm, 470 nm, 470 nm, 510 nm, 500 nm). The emission centers of B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, O-CDs, R-CDs are at 450 nm, 514 nm, 552 nm, 576 nm, 598 nm. The PL spectrum confirms the reality that the fluorescence of M-CDs covers the spectrum ranges from 450 to 650 nm., which is beneficial for forming warm white light with high CRI. In addition, as shown in Fig. 2b, the UV–vis spectrum of MCD shows a distinctly broad absorption band, indicating that M-CDs can be excited at more wavelengths. More importantly, the overlap between fluorescence emission and UV absorption of M-CDs are very small, which is more conducive to the effective fluorescence emission of M-CDs (Figure S2). [32,33] M-CDs exhibit different characteristic absorption peaks in the range of 285 ∼ 530 nm, indicating that CDs have different surface states and their emission may attributed to the excitation of electrons in the n-π* transition of CC/C = O radiation recombination [34]. Figure S3 clearly shows the change of PL emission of B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, O-CDs and R-CDs at a temperature of 20 ∼ 70 °C (solvent: EtOH) (Figure S3). Apart from the FWHM, the FL peak wavelength of the M-CDs also decreases slightly about enhancing temperature, that be attributed to thermally activated exciton dissociation and no radiation trapping [35]. Importantly, the PL intensity of M-CDs decays less than 15 % at 70 °C, which indicates that the fluorescence of M-CDs still has excellent stability under higher heat conditions. [36] Raman spectra as shown in Fig. 2c, exhibit two broad peaks at 1327 and 1507 cm−1, which are characterized by the D and G bands of the M-CDs. Respectively corresponding to disordered sp3 carbon and graphitized sp2 carbon, this is related to the degree of graphitization of the M-CDs. The value of IG/ID is about 1.012, 1.061, 1.124, 1.223 and 1.261 for B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, O-CDs, and R-CDs, respectively. The reason for the increase in the value of IG/ID is the increase in the degree of graphitization, which is related to the emission red shift of CDs. The increasing values are corresponding to the increase of graphitization degree, which are related to the red shift emission of the five samples, and thus further confirm the high graphitization and large conjugate area of the M-CDs. [37] Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) of the M-CDs are shown in Fig. 2d. It is worth noting that CDs with different emission colors all show similar FT-IR spectra, proving that they have the similar functional group. The strong stretching vibration bands of OH, CC, CC and CO, C –H characteristic of the M-CDs are also observed at 3335, 1630, 1410, 1160 and 650 cm−1, respectively.

The results of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) proved that all CDs contained only three elements of C, H, and O, and further verified infrared data (Figure S5). Moreover, with the fluorescence of M-CDs is red-shifted, its oxygen content also increases, which is the result of dehydration condensation of more phenols, and it also shows that the CDs with long wavelength emission have larger conjugate areas (insets of Figure S5). The high-resolution XPS C1s spectrum of M-CDs is shown in Figs. 3 a-e, the C1s band can be deconvoluted into three peaks with 284.0 eV, 284.8 eV, 285.7 eV, corresponding to CC, CC and CO . The O1s band can be deconvoluted into two peaks with 531.7 eV and 534.8 eV, corresponding to CO and CO. This result further illustrates that they contain the same chemical bonds (Figure S6). [38,39] It can be seen from the figure that the content of CC/C = C is the largest, which means that the core of M-CDs is composed of a conjugated structure of carbon, which shows that our guess on the formation process of carbon dots is correct. Both infrared data and XPS data prove that the carbon dots have the same elements and similar chemical structures. This result says that due to the limitation of quantum effects, the optical properties of MCD are determined by the size of its conjugate area. In addition, the Zeta potential of CDs is shown in Fig. 3f (pH = 7.0). The zeta potentials of B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, O-CDs, and R-CDs were -40.92 mV, -43.95 mV, -48.62 mV, -51.64 mV, -54.42 mV, respectively. The zeta potential values of M-CDs are all negative, indicating that there are hydroxyl groups at the edge. At the same time, with red shift of the fluorescence, the zeta potential decreases due to the more hydroxyl groups at the edge of the CDs with longer emission wavelengths. These show that the long-wavelength emission carbon dots are caused by more monomer dehydration condensation become. This is consistent with our inference that the red shift of CDs fluorescence is affected by their conjugate area.

The UV–vis spectra of B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, O-CDs and R-CDs indicated that they have independent absorption in the range of 280 ∼ 540 nm in addition to similar UV absorption at 210 ∼ 240 nm (Figure S2). The UV–vis feature absorption peaks of B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, O-CDs and R-CDs are at 292, 468, 484, 516 and 536 nm, which indicates that the CDs with different emission colors have different sizes of conjugate areas. The Fluorescence of M-CDs can be excited with a wide range of wavelengths and the maximum emission peak position is virtually independent of the excitation wavelength, showed the property of exciting independent emission (Fig. 4 a-e). This result shown that all the CDs are uniform in surface and have the similar UV–vis absorption structure and fluorescence emission center. Ultimately, the QYs of five samples are measured to be 50.54 %, 41.21 %, 38.8 %, 36.92 % and 23.85 % (Figure S7), respectively. In addition, the PL spectrum shows that the peak intensity of the CDs fluorescence is related to the corresponding excitation wavelength, which indicates that the emission of the CDs based on the decay of the band-edge exciton. [40,41] What is more exciting is that after using a UV light source (λ = 365 nm) for 9 h of continuous irradiation, the fluorescence of the CDs showed only a small attenuation (< 15 %), indicating that M-CDs have high stability (Figure S8). Meanwhile, the solution concentration was changed at the same time, the fluorescence emission position of M-CDs also hardly changed (Figure S9). The real image of M-CDs (solvent: EtOH) under sunlight and UV light is shown in Fig. 4f. The strong fluorescence emission from blue to red under UV light (λ = 365 nm) can be clearly observed. The above results include FT-IR, Raman, XPS, UV–vis spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy, which clearly show that the core of M-CDs is composed of a conjugated structure of CC/C = C and the conjugate area of the B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, O-CDs and R-CDs gradually increases, that is size increases. The increase in size reduces the energy gaps of CDs, leading to a red shift of the emission wavelength, which is mainly due to the band gap transition of the conjugated p domain in the sp2 carbon core. [42] Besides, the oxygen content and CC/C = C functional group are accompanied by the increase in the conjugated area. the CC groups with stronger electron-withdrawing ability can introduce more new energy levels into the sp2 conjugated domain, which results reduce the HOMO-LUMO gaps, increase the electronic transition and emission red-shifted of the CDs. [43] Therefore, based on this fact, we speculate that the red-shifted fluorescence of BCD, G-CDs, Y-CDs, O-CDs and R-CDs is related to the increase in conjugated area and CC/C = C groups. Furthermore, the specific energy levels diagram and schematic illustrations with electronic transition process diagram of M-CDs are illustrated in Fig. 5 .

The DFT theoretical calculations further confirmed our conjecture: a. the pure electron donor hydroxyl groups at the edge and high graphitization degree of CDs are the main reasons for its better stability; b. the main reason for the red shift of M-CDs fluorescence is that the conjugate area of CDs gradually increases, and the conjugated area and CC groups increase. The increase of oxygen content and CC groups can introduce more new energy levels in the conjugated structure of CDs, which leads to more electronic transitions and reduces its HOMO-LUMO gap. We use 6, 10, 19, 20 and 24 fused benzene rings polygonal conjugated structures consisting by electron donor hydroxyl groups to simulate B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, O-CDs and R-CDs. DFT calculations were performed in Gauss software, 6−311G** is selected as base group and simulation is carried out in EtOH solution. The calculation results are shown in Fig. 6 . The degree of delocalization of the HOMO and LUMO can be qualitatively judged, for instance, by their corresponding electron cloud density distributions around the whole molecular structure [44,45] In this sense, a more uniformly distributed electron cloud density would indicate a higher degree of delocalization. Obviously, it can be seen from the calculation results that the electron cloud density of HOMO and LUMO of B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, O-CDs and R-CDs are evenly distributed, which indicates that the structure of our simulation is consistent with the theory. Differences in energy levels between HOMO and LUMO of B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, O-CDs, R-CDs are -0.14217 eV, -0.11688 eV, -0.075671 eV, -0.03203 eV, -0.02765 eV, respectively. Obviously, from B-CDs to R-CDs, the energy level difference between HOMO and LUMO is gradually smaller, which is consistent with the theory that the smaller the gap between HOMO and LUMO, greater the red-shifted of the emission with CDs [46]. That result further proves that the fluorescence red shift of CDs is caused by the increase of oxygen element and the increase of conjugate area.

These carefully designed theoretical calculations show that the polygonal structure and the electron donor hydroxyl groups play an important role in determining the color purity and stability of M-CDs. Firstly, the higher graphitization degree leads to higher stability structural of M-CDs, which greatly reduces electron-phonon coupling. As shown in the temperature-dependent PL spectrum, this contributes to the PL spectrum of M-CDs with exciton emission with wide FWHMs. [47] And mixing emitters with wide but different emission spectra to obtain white light emission is a favorable approach. Secondly, the pure electron donor hydroxyl group at the edge position of M-CDs can also greatly increase the π electron cloud density and promote the pure radiative recombination of constrained electrons and holes [48]. Thirdly, the increase of the conjugate area of the carbon dot increases the electron delocalization of the conjugate system, the transition energy decreases, and the fluorescence red-shift. In summary, theoretical studies have shown that the functionalization of the hydroxyl group of the electron donor and the increase in the conjugate area make the CDs show a highly delocalized charge, excellent structural stability, and reduced electron-phonon coupling, which are play an important role in M-CDs showing fluorescence red-shift emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of G-CDs and Y-CDs to ClO−
ce:section.ce:para: The research results of the detection performance of G-CDs and Y-CDs are as follows. As shown in Fig. 7 a & b, the fluorescence of G-CDs and Y-CDs was obviously quenched by ClO−, and no significant decrease was observed for other ions. These suggest that G-CDs and Y-CDs have excellent selectivity towards to ClO−. Subsequently, to explore the anti-interference ability of G-CDs and Y-CDs towards ClO−, the competition experiments were carried out. As depicted in Fig. 7c&d, when there are only other anions, the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs and Y-CDs is almost unchanged, at ClO- coexists with other ions, a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity can be observed. The results illustrate that influence of potential interference on ClO− detection is negligible at the same concentration. The above results indicated that prepared G-CDs and Y-CDs possessed highly sensitivity and selective for ClO− detection, and have great application potential for the detection of ClO− in complex environments.

In addition, to study the quantitative ability of G-CDs and Y-CDs to ClO−, the sensitivity tests of G-CDs and Y-CDs toward different concentrations of ClO− were carried out. It can be obtained from Fig. 8 a & b that with the increase of ClO− concentration, the fluorescence emission of G-CDs and Y-CDs continuously and significantly decreases. The CIE chromaticity coordinates of G-CDs and Y-CDs displayed from dark to pale (Figure S10). What’s more, the curve of fluorescence titration revealed that the change of fluorescence intensities of G-CDs and Y-CDs (F0-F) have excellent linearity with the concentration of ClO− in scope of 0 ∼ 50 μM and 4 ∼ 44 μM, respectively (linear equation: GF0-F = 37.5877Cg + 42.8336, R2 = 0.99766; YF0-F = 25.3856Cy + 177.2267, R2 = 0.99074) (Fig. 8c & d). According to the formula LOD = 3σ/k (σ is the standard deviation of the fluorescence intensity of 11 blank samples; k is the slope of the linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and ClO- concentration), the detection limits of G-CDs and Y-CDs for ClO− was found to be as low as 31.1 nM and 56.5 nM. These results demonstrated that G-CDs and Y-CDs were highly sensitive "off" fluorescent sensors for ClO−.

Moreover, the affect of pH in the detection of ClO− was explored (Figure S11). Free G-CDs and Y-CDs have good stability in the pH range of 5 ∼ 10, and they have also been observed to show excellent responses to ClO− in the pH range of 3 ∼ 11, which indicates that they could be applied to detect ClO− in a large pH range. As shown in Figure S12, the changes in fluorescence intensity (F0-F) of G-CDs and Y-CDs were almost negligible in the absence of ClO−, revealing the photostability of the sensors were very excellent. When 50 μM ClO− is added to the probe solution, the change of fluorescence intensity (F0-F) reached a stable level within 20 s. Hereafter, we characterize the obtained reaction mixture of sensors and ClO− through UV–vis spectrum and fluorescence lifetime to making prediction of the detection mechanism. After the addition of ClO−, the UV absorption peak of the probe was significantly weakened or even disappeared, indicating that the probe and ClO− formed a non-luminescent whole through weak combination, which is the expression of static quenching (Fig. 9 a&b). [49] To evaluate the “core state” quenching effect of CDs in detail, the fluorescence lifetime of G-CDs and Y-CDs in the presence and absence of ClO− were measured, these curves were fitted by a double exponential function (Fig. 9c&d). In the dynamic quenching process caused by fluorescence resonance energy transfer, the addition of quenching agent will cause a sharp change in fluorescence lifetime. Adding ClO− results in only a small lifetime change (τ0/τ ≈ 1) in this study, which shows that there is almost no fluorescence resonance energy transfer in this system. [50] In addition, the possibility of internal filter effect (IFE) can also be excluded between CDs and CIO-. This is because the emission wavelength of G-CDs and Y-CDs is above 510 nm, and the absorptions of ClO- was very weak in this wavelength range, which means no remarkable overlaps between the emission of CDs and absorption of ClO-. Meanwhile the UV absorption of ClO- has almost no overlap with the characteristic absorption peaks of G-CDs&Y-CDs. (Figure S13). Therefore, the IFE may happen with a very low frequency. [51]

A further analysis was conducted using the Stern-Volmer model. [52] where F0 and F is the fluorescence intensity of sensors in the absence and presence of ClO−, respectively; Ksv is the Stern − Volmer quenching constant; [Q] is the concentration of ClO−; Kq is the quenching rate constant, and τ0 is the fluorescence lifetime of G-CDs and Y-CDs, with 3.9694 ns and 3.1874 ns, respectively. The analysis results are shown in Table 3.
(1)
F 0
/
F = 1 + K s v
Q
= 1 + K q
τ 0
Q

As shown in Table 3, as the temperature increases, the Stern-Volmer quenching constant (Ksv) decreases, and the quenching rate constant (Kq) is much larger than the maximum diffusion collision quenching constant (2.0 × 1010 L mol−1·s−1). It can be speculated that the fluorescence quenching of G-CDs&Y-CDs is due to the static complex formed by the combination of G-CDs&Y-CDs and ClO-, which is verified dynamic quenching is highly unlikely to happen in the detection system of this study. Based on the above description, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of G-CDs and Y-CDs after adding ClO- is a static quenching. Based on this, describing the possible response mechanism of sensors for detecting ClO- (Fig. 9e). In addition, fluorescence detection of the analyte in the actual sample is the main way to further prove the practical application value of the sensor. [53] Based on this fact, we used G-CDs and Y-CDs to carry out the spiked recovery experiments on ClO- in campus lake water, tap water and fetal bovine serum. As illustrate in Table 1 , the recovery values of ClO- were 98.04 % ∼ 105.42 % in three samples. It demonstrated that G-CDs and Y-CDs had the potential capability to analyze ClO- in environment water and fetal bovine serum samples. Meanwhile, this work was compared with other methods of detecting ClO-, as shown in Table 2
. The G-CDs and Y-CDs exhibited wider detection range than other methods and have low detection limit, which can provide better analysis method for ClO- detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in fluorescent films and white LEDs
ce:section.ce:para: The remarkable optical performance of M-CD encouraged us to further exploit them application in fluorescent films and LEDs in order to develop the next generation display technology. We have chosen different solid substrates to make composites to meet the needs of various applications. PMMA with high transparency and easy processing properties is used as a solid matrix. Based on this, we prepared CDs/PMMA fluorescence films (Fig. 10 a). In sunlight, CDs/PMMA films with excellent transparencyemit blue, green, yellow, orange, and red solid fluorescence, respectively. This result indicated that the CDs/PMMA films have ability to resist self-quenching. At the same time, under 10 h of continuous ultraviolet light irradiation and the temperature increased to 80 °C, the fluorescence intensity decay rate of CDs/PMMA films was less than 20 %, which indicated that CDs/PMMA films have good photothermal stability (Figure S14). Therefore, these composite materials are reasonable candidates for most optical devices. The PL emission spectrum of CDs/PMMA resin films is shown in Fig. 10f, which indicated that the CDs are well dispersed into the PMMA matrix.

Besides, epoxy resin with thermosetting are generally used as matrix, and widely used in the packaging of LED chips. Based on this situation, we used o-Phthalic anhydride as a curing agent to synthesize CDs/epoxy films (Fig. 10b). The CDs/epoxy composite material have a diameter of approximately 2.0 cm and thickness of approximately 2.0 mm. Compared to CDs/PMMA films, CDs/epoxy resin films show higher transparency in daylight, but likewise they also show different emission colors from blue to red under UV light. This result proves that M-CDs can also be well dispersed in epoxy resin.

Profiting from CDs/epoxy resin films have high transparency and thermal stability, we use the prepared it’s for packaging LEDs chips. The B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, O-CDs, and R-CDs were mixed in an appropriate ratio to prepare M-CDs/epoxy resin films, which were then coated on LED chips to produce white-color Emitting LED optics (Fig. 10c). The schematic diagram of the 3D warm color WLED is shown in the Fig. 10d, the bottom is the UV-LED chip, and the top is the M-CDs/epoxy mixed layer. The stability of 3D warm WLEDs is studied via measuring its emission spectra at different working times. As illustrate in Figure S15, 3D warm WLEDs still showed good stability after working for 10 h. Under UV light (λ = 365 nm), the color coordinate of the M-CDs / epoxy composite in the CIE color space (0.32, 0.31) is very close to pure white light (0.33, 0.33), the CCT is 6072 K and CRI is 85.2. (Fig. 10e). Theoretically, the optical devices required by various industries can be prepared by adjusting the content of CDs of different luminescent colors.

In summary, we used phenol derivatives (catechol, m-dihydroxybenzene, hydroquinone, 1,6-dihydroxynaphthalene) as materials to obtain M-CDs by solvothermal method. The construction of M-CDs are concerning to the hydroxyl position on the phenolic substance and the heating times. The specific formation process is: the −OH groups on the raw materials undergo continuous dehydration and condensation processes under the dual effects of concentrated sulfuric acid and heating to form CDs. Through detailed characterization and careful theoretical calculations, it was proved that high graphitization and increase in conjugate area and oxygen element are the main reasons for the unique PL characteristics of M-CDs. Moreover, we examined the difference in detection of non-metal ions by G-CDs and Y-CDs. The results show that CDs have wide detection range, high accuracy, rapid reaction to ClO−, and can be used as fluorescence sensors for ClO− ions detection. In addition, M-CDs can be used for fluorescent films and white-light LEDs due to excellent photoluminescence characteristics. In short, this work would provide a new platform for fluorescence sensing, and provide new ideas for the development of warm-color WLEDs.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Fanyong Yan is a professor in the school of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Tiangong University (PR China). He received his PhD degree from Tianjin University in 2007. His research interests focus on the design and synthesis of molecular probes and sensing materials for temperature-responsive and optical sensors.
Hao zhang is a graduate student at Tiangong University.
Nanhai Yu si a student at Tiangong University.
Zhonghui Sun is a graduate student at Tiangong University.
Liang Chen is a professor in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing (PR China).
